entries.notes: Foundation entry for the entire Neocaridina davidi color-morph complex (hybrid consolidation policy). When researched, populate alsoKnownAs with: Red Cherry, Sakura Cherry, Painted Fire Red, Bloody Mary, Yellow Neon/Sunkist, Green Jade, Blue Dream, Blue Velvet, Blue Diamond, Black Rose, Red/Blue/Carbon Rili, Snow White, Orange Pumpkin, Chocolate; also older synonym Neocaridina heteropoda. All color morphs share identical care; one entry covers them all.

Covers the entire C. cantonensis Bee/Crystal complex. alsoKnownAs should capture: Crystal Black, Bee/Red Bee/Black Bee, Golden Bee, Snow White Bee, Tiger Bee, Tangerine Tiger, Pinto, Galaxy + grades S/SS/SSS/Mosura. Taxonomy note: some sources reassign Crystals to Caridina logemanni — research agent should record both attributions.

Separated from C. cantonensis Crystal complex per hybrid policy: distinct softer-water and TDS sensitivity. alsoKnownAs: King Kong, Panda, Blue Bolt, Wine Red, Red Wine, Black King Kong, Shadow Mosura, Extreme Black King Kong. Taxonomy is fluid — some sources still classify as C. cantonensis hybrids; medium taxonFit confidence.

alsoKnownAs: Yamato Shrimp, Japanese Algae Shrimp, Algae-Eating Shrimp, older synonym Caridina japonica. Distinct from Neocaridina/Caridina-cantonensis complexes — larvae require brackish water to develop, does not breed in pure FW. Standalone entry.
alsoKnownAs: Sulawesi Cardinal, Red Polka Dot. Sulawesi-endemic biotope (pH 7.8-8.4, temp 27-30°C, GH 4-6 with high TDS). Species-only tank strongly recommended.
alsoKnownAs: Six-Banded Sulawesi, Harlequin Shrimp (FW — flag common-name collision with the saltwater Hymenocera picta). Lake Towuti endemic. Care parallels C. dennerli.
alsoKnownAs: White-Spotted Tiger Sulawesi, Tiger Lake Shrimp. Lake Matano endemic. Care parallels other Sulawesi Caridina.

alsoKnownAs: Tiger Shrimp, Orange-Eye Blue Tiger, Royal Blue Tiger, Black Tiger, Red Tiger, Super Tiger; older taxonomy Caridina cf. cantonensis 'Tiger'. Tiger shrimp lineage is taxonomically debated — long treated as C. cf. cantonensis 'Tiger', now generally placed under C. mariae. Slightly more tolerant than Crystals but still softer-water preference.
alsoKnownAs: Caridina babaulti, Green Shrimp, Malaya Shrimp, Blue Malaya, Stripe Malaya, Indian Zebra Shrimp. Color-changing Caridina from India; tolerant of a wider parameter range than Crystals.

alsoKnownAs: Wood Shrimp, Asian Filter Shrimp, Singapore Flower Shrimp, Fan Shrimp. Distinct biology: filter-feeder with fan-like front appendages, needs steady current and fine particulate food. Larvae require brackish water; will not breed in FW.

alsoKnownAs: African Fan Shrimp, Gabon Shrimp, Giant African Filter Shrimp, Blue Rhino Shrimp, Cameroon Shrimp. Filter-feeder reaching 10-15 cm. Cryptic — often hidden by day. Larvae require brackish water; effectively impossible to breed in FW.

alsoKnownAs: Glass Shrimp, Grass Shrimp, Eastern Grass Shrimp, Palaemon paludosus (alt genus placement). Sold both as display species and as feeder shrimp. Hardy, breeds in pure FW (unlike Atyidae filter-feeders). Trade 'ghost shrimp' may include several Palaemonetes species — flag uncertainty in research.

alsoKnownAs: White-Cherry Shrimp (misnomer), Asian Whisker Shrimp, Riceland Prawn. Commonly misidentified and mis-sold as ghost shrimp; semi-aggressive with elongated claws. Will eat smaller shrimp and small fish.

alsoKnownAs: Malaysian Prawn, Scampi, Cherabin. Reaches 30+ cm. Predatory and territorial; species tank required at adult size. Larvae need brackish water to develop. Primarily an aquaculture species but kept ornamentally.

alsoKnownAs: Red Claw River Prawn, Brown Arm Shrimp (some sources). Southeast Asian Macrobrachium reaching ~15 cm. Semi-aggressive — may fight with smaller invertebrates and fish. Aqua Imports has a serviceable care page (pH 6.8-7.5, 70-78°F, 20 gal). Distinct from M. asperulum 'Bee Mountain' (Taiwanese, smaller). Research agent should clarify trade-name overlap.
alsoKnownAs: Blue Crayfish, Florida Crayfish, Sapphire Crayfish, Everglades Crayfish. Selective-bred blue color morph of a Florida native. Adult ~10 cm. Aggressive toward tank-mates; escape artist.

alsoKnownAs: Louisiana Crayfish, Crawdad, Mudbug, Ghost Crayfish (white morph), Tangerine Lobster (orange morph), White Specter (color morph). Globally invasive — banned for sale or possession in many jurisdictions. Trade includes several color morphs sold under brand names; consolidated to species entry per policy. Research must include legal-status caveat.

alsoKnownAs: Marmorkrebs, Self-Cloning Crayfish, Procambarus fallax f. virginalis. All-female parthenogenetic species; every individual is a clone. Banned in EU and several US states due to invasive risk. Research must include legal-status caveat.
alsoKnownAs: Common Yabby, Australian Yabby, Blue Yabby (color morph). Australian native; cool-water tolerant. Adult ~15-20 cm. Blue color morph commonly traded.
alsoKnownAs: Red Claw, Tropical Blue Lobster, Queensland Red Claw. Tropical Australian/PNG Cherax; adults to 25 cm. Males develop the trademark red patch on the claws.

alsoKnownAs: Holthuis' Crayfish, Crazy Patrick (trade morph), Hoa Creek (trade morph). Indonesian/Papuan Cherax. Trade morphs (Apricot, Hoa Creek, Crazy Patrick) lumped under this entry. Some morphs may belong to congeners — research agent should flag uncertainty.

alsoKnownAs: CPO, CPO Crayfish, Mexican Dwarf Crayfish, Patzcuaro Crayfish, Orange Dwarf Crayfish. Dwarf to ~4-5 cm; the most community-friendly crayfish. Orange morph dominates the trade — wild-type C. patzcuarensis is brown/mottled.
alsoKnownAs: Swamp Dwarf Crayfish, Shufeldt's Dwarf Crayfish. Native US Gulf coast dwarf crayfish; brown-gray with darker striping.
alsoKnownAs: Dwarf Crayfish (general trade), Mosquito Crayfish. One of the smallest dwarf crayfish; adults ~2.5 cm.
alsoKnownAs: Aztec Dwarf Crayfish, Acocil. Mexican high-altitude dwarf crayfish; cooler-water tolerant.
alsoKnownAs: Matano Crab, Sulawesi Purple Crab, Orange-Claw Crab. Fully aquatic Sulawesi-endemic crab. Same biotope demands as Sulawesi Caridina (high pH/hardness, warm water).
alsoKnownAs: Sulawesi Panther Crab, Spotted Sulawesi Crab. Fully aquatic Sulawesi crab with leopard-like spot pattern. Predatory — not safe with small fish or shrimp.

alsoKnownAs: Pill-Box Crab, Micro Crab, Hairy Micro Crab, False Spider Crab. Fully aquatic Thai endemic; adult ~1 cm carapace. Among the few aquarium FW crabs genuinely safe in community shrimp/nano tanks — peaceful filter-feeder, cryptic. Soft-water Thai stream biotope (pH 6.5-7.5, temp 22-26°C).

alsoKnownAs: Pom Pom Crab (FW — flag common-name collision with marine Lybia tessellata), Bearded Pom Pom Crab. Fully aquatic Asian crab with characteristic setae tufts ("pom poms") on the chelae used for filter-feeding. Small (~2 cm carapace), peaceful, suitable for nano community tanks with shrimp and small fish.

alsoKnownAs: Scud, Sideswimmer, Freshwater Shrimp (UK colloquial — not a true shrimp), Gammarus. European amphipod commonly cultured as live food for predatory fish. Display use limited but legitimate in coldwater/temperate setups.
alsoKnownAs: Aztec Scud, Sideswimmer, Hyalella. Widespread North American amphipod. Often introduced as cleanup-crew detritivore as much as live food.

alsoKnownAs: Water Hoglouse, Water Sow Bug, Asellus. Truly aquatic FW isopod (Crustacea, Isopoda). Cultured as detritivore and live food. Distinct from terrestrial Porcellio isopods despite shared common-name family.

alsoKnownAs: Daphnia, Common Water Flea, Large Daphnia, Daphnia pulex (smaller congener). Cultured as live food, not displayed. Entry frames husbandry from the culture-keeper perspective. Covers both D. magna and D. pulex as the common 'daphnia' culture organisms.
alsoKnownAs: Russian Water Flea, Tropical Daphnia, Microcrustacean (trade). Smaller, warmer-water alternative to Daphnia; preferred for raising fry that can't fit larger Daphnia.
